coredata.dc:description: Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), a biodegradable polymer, has attracted considerable interest in the food packaging industry. However, the efficacy of PVA films as food packaging materials is limited owing to their inherently highlight transmittance, hydrophilicity, and solubility in water. Emerging two-dimensional materials, particularly transition metal dichalcogenides, exhibit significant potential in enhancing the mechanical, thermal, optical, ultraviolet-blocking, and oxygen barrier properties of polymer-based nanocomposites. In this study, few-layered water-dispersible MoS2 nanosheets were synthesized via ultrasonic mixing and liquid exfoliation and integrated with Ag-doped nitrogen-functionalized carbon quantum dots (Ag-NCQDs), referred to as MoS2-Ag-NCQDs. Incorporating only 1 wt% of MoS2-Ag-NCQDs into a PVA matrix resulted in notable improvements in crystallinity, thermal stability, and oxygen barrier properties. The improved crystallinity of the polymer matrix constrained the gas molecule mobility by increasing diffusion pathways and lowering the permeation rates. Notably, the incorporation of MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials significantly enhanced the oxygen barrier performance, yielding a 99.8 % reduction in the oxygen transmission rate with that of pure PVA films. This remarkable improvement is attributed to the enhanced crystallinity, high aspect ratio, and superior exfoliation of the MoS2 nanosheets, which facilitate effective interactions with gas molecules and impede their movement. Furthermore, the PVA/MoS2-Ag-NCQDs nanocomposite films significantly improved ginger's preservation and nutritional integrity, thereby extending its shelf life. This study highlights the potential of MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials for improving oxygen barrier performance, advancing pharmaceutical applications, and enhancing ultraviolet shielding when used in food packaging systems.

head.ce:title: Preparation of MoS2 modified with carbon quantum dots and its application to extremely high oxygen-barrier nanocomposite films for packaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), a biodegradable polymer, has attracted considerable interest in the food packaging industry. However, the efficacy of PVA films as food packaging materials is limited owing to their inherently highlight transmittance, hydrophilicity, and solubility in water. Emerging two-dimensional materials, particularly transition metal dichalcogenides, exhibit significant potential in enhancing the mechanical, thermal, optical, ultraviolet-blocking, and oxygen barrier properties of polymer-based nanocomposites. In this study, few-layered water-dispersible MoS2 nanosheets were synthesized via ultrasonic mixing and liquid exfoliation and integrated with Ag-doped nitrogen-functionalized carbon quantum dots (Ag-NCQDs), referred to as MoS2-Ag-NCQDs. Incorporating only 1 wt% of MoS2-Ag-NCQDs into a PVA matrix resulted in notable improvements in crystallinity, thermal stability, and oxygen barrier properties. The improved crystallinity of the polymer matrix constrained the gas molecule mobility by increasing diffusion pathways and lowering the permeation rates. Notably, the incorporation of MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials significantly enhanced the oxygen barrier performance, yielding a 99.8 % reduction in the oxygen transmission rate with that of pure PVA films. This remarkable improvement is attributed to the enhanced crystallinity, high aspect ratio, and superior exfoliation of the MoS2 nanosheets, which facilitate effective interactions with gas molecules and impede their movement. Furthermore, the PVA/MoS2-Ag-NCQDs nanocomposite films significantly improved ginger's preservation and nutritional integrity, thereby extending its shelf life. This study highlights the potential of MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials for improving oxygen barrier performance, advancing pharmaceutical applications, and enhancing ultraviolet shielding when used in food packaging systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Two-dimensional (2D) materials, particularly transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs), have attracted considerable interest from the scientific community following the groundbreaking discovery of graphene (Thakur et al., 2018). These materials have been extensively studied for their potential in polymer reinforcement owing to their unique crystalline structures and superior physical properties. WS2 and MoS2 are prominent TMDs, and are distinguished by their unique electronic, optical, and mechanical characteristics that further enhance their application prospects in advanced materials science (Wang et al., 2015). Particularly, MoS2 exhibits superior mechanical properties, making it an effective reinforcement agent for enhancing the performance of various polymers across numerous applications (Castellanos-Gomez et al., 2012). One of the most promising applications of polymer composites incorporating 2D nanomaterials is the development of transparent, flexible, and cost-effective super gas barrier films (Dou et al., 2014; Layek et al., 2014). The high aspect ratio of these 2D nanomaterials is crucial because it allows them to function as physical barriers that impede the diffusion of gas molecules, thereby improving the overall barrier properties of the material (Liu et al., 2016).

The incorporation of impermeable nanoplatelets such as graphene, boron nitride, and nanoclays within a polymer matrix enhances the gas barrier properties by constraining the gas diffusion pathways. The high aspect ratios of these nanosheets create extensive tortuous paths for gas molecules, resulting in low permeability (Nguyen et al., 2017). According to Kim et al., 2D MoS2 outperforms WS2 in enhancing the mechanical properties of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), making it a strong candidate for barrier enhancement in polymer nanocomposites owing to its substantial surface area and 2D structure (Kim et al., 2014). Moreover, the fluorescence of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be improved via heteroatom doping, thereby expanding their applications to analytical fields and food packaging (Ahmed et al., 2024). Among these precursors, chitosan, a nitrogen-rich polysaccharide, was used to effectively synthesize nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) because of its polymeric structure, which supports a coherent graphite framework (Song et al., 2018). However, the available literature on the barrier properties of MoS2-based chitosan-derived NCQDs for food packaging applications is limited. Hydrothermal carbonization is a green and sustainable method for deriving CQDs from biomass and is known for its simplicity and environmental benefits (Bhandari et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2024; Kumar et al., 2018). Biomass precursors are cost-effective and eco-friendly and are thus ideal for developing advanced fluorescent materials (Zhao et al., 2019).

PVA is a biodegradable polymer that has garnered significant attention because of its diverse applications and utility in films and food packaging (Yang et al., 2016). Its desirable properties include nontoxicity, biodegradability, hydrophilicity, superior film-forming capabilities, transparency, mechanical strength, and high oxygen barrier performance (Xu et al., 2019). However, the low ultraviolet (UV) barrier, hydrophilicity, and solubility in water limit the efficacy of PVA films as food packaging materials, impeding its broad-scale application (Chen et al., 2018). Therefore, the development of functional PVA films has emerged as a critical research area, aimed at enhancing the applicability of these materials. A promising strategy involves the incorporation of nanomaterials into PVA matrices, which has been shown to significantly improve the performance of packaging materials (Hu et al., 2016). The PVA structure is characterized by hydrogen-bonding functionalities, which allow its integration with various active nanomaterials, enabling the formation of new hydrogen bonds and enhancing the overall functional properties (Toyoda & Yamamoto, 2018).

The formation of complexes with CQDs is an effective approach to enhance the colorimetric properties of metal nanoparticles, which are quasi-spherical, 0D nanomaterials with typical diameters of less than 10 nm. In recent years, interest in CQDs has surged owing to their unique attributes that are caused by structural defects and quantum confinement effects, which are inherent at the nanoscale. Introducing heteroatoms, such as oxygen or nitrogen, into CQDs allows for the precise tuning of their electronic and optical properties for targeted applications (Horo et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2019). The presence of various functional groups on CQDs as well as their exceptional electrical conductivity and optical stability position them as promising candidates for indicator systems. When integrated with noble metal nanoparticles, CQDs offer markedly enhanced electronic characteristics and optimized localized surface plasmon resonance features, showing significantly improved performance for various packaging applications (Lohrasbi Nejad & Shekarchizadeh, 2024).

This paper presents a novel approach—using MoS2-nanosheet-integrated Ag-doped NCQDs (MoS2-Ag-NCQDs)—to enhance PVA films for their use in active food packaging aimed at extending shelf life. PVA-based nanocomposite films with improved barrier properties were synthesized, and their chemical and physical characteristics were analyzed. The interactions between MoS2-Ag-NCQDs and the hydroxyl groups in PVA facilitated chain segment formation, leading to unique deformation mechanisms under stress. The oxygen transmission rate (OTR) was significantly reduced, reflecting enhanced barrier properties. The efficacy of these films as UV-protective coatings was demonstrated by improving the postharvest stability of ginger exposed to UV radiation. This study underscores the potential of MoS2-Ag-NCQDs for enhancing the properties of packaging materials.

Silver nitrate, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), and commercial-grade PVA (MW: 78,000 g/mol, degree of hydrolysis: 98.0 %–99.5 %) were supplied by OCI Co. (Incheon, South Korea); acetic acid and chitosan (MW = 190,000–310,000 Da) were purchased from Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co., Ltd. (Seoul, South Korea). MoS2 (99 % metal basis, Alfa Aesar, Great Britain) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). All analytical-grade reagents were used as received without further purification. Ginger was purchased from a local farm in Wonju, South Korea.

Bulk MoS2 was efficiently exfoliated into nanosheets via a synergistic approach involving PVA and CTAB using an optimized ultrahigh-power sonicator. Briefly, 1 g of bulk MoS2 with 1 g each of PVA and CTAB were dispersed in 100 mL of deionized water in a beaker. The suspension was then sonicated for 8 h using a Powersonic 610 sonicator with an output power of 400 W. The sonication process induced notable increments in both the temperature and pressure within the mixture owing to solvodynamic forces, which effectively disrupted the van der Waals interactions between the MoS2 layers and facilitated their exfoliation. The solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm and the supernatant was collected for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NCQDs and Ag-doped NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Ag-doped NCQDs (Ag-NCQDs) were synthesized via a hydrothermal process. A solution was prepared by dissolving 0.40 g of AgNO3 and 1 g of chitosan in a 100 mL aqueous solution of acetic acid (1 wt%). The mixture was stirred for 4 h to ensure homogeneity. Subsequently, 70 mL of this solution was transferred to a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor and heated in an autoclave at 200 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the resulting products were cooled to ambient temperature. The resultant product was filtered using Whatman paper with a pore size of 0.2 µm and then centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 20 min to eliminate any residual precipitates. The concentration of Ag-NCQDs in a solution was quantified by UV–visible spectrophotometry. The concentration of Ag-NCQD solution (1.05 mg/mL) was determined using the calibration curve derived from standard measurements (Section S5, Supplementary Materials). An analogous procedure, excluding the Ag precursors, was adopted to synthesize NCQDs. The resulting yellow colloidal solution was stored until further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MoS2-NCQD and MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Suspensions of NCQDs and Ag-NCQDs (10 mL) were individually mixed with MoS2 sheet suspensions (10 mL) and magnetically stirred for 3 h to obtain a homogeneous mixture. Subsequently, the MoS2-NCQD and MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials were precipitated, and an excess amount of ethanol was used to remove unbound NCQDs, Ag-NCQDs, and surplus surfactants (PVA and CTAB). The resulting precipitates were redispersed in deionized water via ultrasonication. The redispersed sample was sonicated for an additional 15 min, and the colloidal suspensions of MoS2-NCQD and MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials were collected for further characterization.

The PVA-based nanocomposite films were fabricated using a solution-casting method. Briefly, 1 wt% suspensions of MoS2, MoS2-NCQDs, and MoS2-Ag-NCQDs were separately prepared by dispersing them in 100 mL of deionized water and stirring for 20 m. Then, 10 g of PVA was incorporated into each suspension, and the mixture was heated at 90 °C in an oil bath with continuous stirring for 3 h. Later, the mixtures were cooled to room temperature and were cast on glass slides using a film coating machine (Kibae & T Co., Ltd., Knife Coating Device KP-3000H, Suwon, South Korea) and dried at 60 °C for 1 h. Three types of PVA-based nanocomposite films were successfully prepared: PVA/MoS2, PVA/MoS2-NCQDs, and PVA/MoS2-Ag-NCQDs. Moreover, a pure PVA film was also prepared using a similar approach. Characterization of the PVA-based nanocomposite films is discussed in Supplementary Section S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy and photoluminescence analyses
ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous suspensions of NCQDs, Ag-NCQDs, MoS2-NCQDs, and MoS2-Ag-NCQDs were prepared via hydrothermal routes, ultrasonication, and liquid exfoliation, followed by incorporation into the PVA matrix to determine their effectiveness in food packaging applications. The microstructures and morphologies of these nanomaterials were analyzed via high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). Fig. 1(a) and (d) show the spherical morphologies of the as-prepared NCQDs and Ag-NCQDs with average diameters of 3 ± 1 and 4 ± 1 nm, respectively; these nanomaterials were well dispersed in the aqueous medium. Moreover, the HRTEM images of NCQDs and Ag-NCQDs reveal lattice spacings of 0.21 and 0.24 nm corresponding to the (100) and (111) planes of NCQDs and Ag, respectively (Ansari, Singh, Mohapatra, et al., 2019; Nguyen et al., 2024). The TEM and HRTEM images illustrating the morphology of MoS2-NCQDs are shown in Fig. 1(b) and (c), whereas those for MoS2-Ag-NCQDs are illustrated in Fig. 1(e) and (f), respectively. The semi-transparent characteristics and active edges observed along the sides of the MoS2 nanosheets indicate that the incorporation of NCQDs and Ag-NCQDs leads to subtle structural modifications in the MoS2 scaffold (Thanh et al., 2014). These nanomaterials appear to be uniformly distributed across the MoS2 nanosheets, suggesting an effective interaction between the components. These findings indicate the successful functionalization of the MoS2 nanosheets via the doping of NCQDs and Ag-NCQDs.

Furthermore, the HRTEM images shown in the insets of Fig. 1(c) and (f) reveal interlayer spacings of 0.22 and 0.62 nm corresponding to the (103) and (002) planes of MoS2, respectively, within the 2D MoS2-based nanocomposites (Hu et al., 2014). Further, selected area electron diffraction (SAED) confirmed the crystallinity of the MoS2-NCQD and MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials. The presence of these nanomaterials without impurities was confirmed by indexing and matching the corresponding SAED patterns with standard databases. Furthermore, the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) elemental mapping images shown in Fig. 1(g) and (h) verify the presence of Mo, S, N, and Ag in the MoS2-Ag-NCQDs nanomaterials. Photoluminescence (PL) measurement was performed to investigate the luminescence characteristics of the NCQD, MoS2-NCQD, and MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials (Supplementary Section S2). The incorporation of MoS2 and Ag-NCQDs significantly reduced the PL peak intensity, indicating improved charge transfer processes and enhanced separation of charge carriers within the MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials (Fig. S1 ).

The interactions between nanomaterials and the PVA matrix were investigated through Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The spectra show the characteristic absorption bands of pure PVA, PVA/MoS2, PVA/MoS2-NCQD, and PVA/MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterial films ( Fig. 2). The analysis revealed that all films exhibited a pronounced absorption peak at 3248–3274 cm–1, which corresponds to the -OH functional group of PVA. Furthermore, peaks due to stretching vibrations were observed at 2908, 1415, and 1087 cm−1, which can attributed to C-H, C-H2, and C-O bonds, respectively (Bao et al., 2011). The peaks identified within the 2800–3000 cm−1 range are indicative of both asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the -CH2 moieties. Notably, the broad band observed between 2980, and 3650 cm−1 demonstrates a redshift and a marked decrease in intensity upon the incorporation of nanomaterials onto the PVA film, suggesting strengthening of the intermolecular hydrogen-bonding interactions between PVA and the embedded nanomaterials.

Further, the peaks observed at 1324 and 1415 cm−1 correspond to the wagging and bending vibrations of the -CH2 group (Krishna et al., 2021). Furthermore, minor shifts are detected in the wavenumber ranges of 1657–1666 and 1087–1093 cm–1, indicating the formation of coordination bonds between the nanomaterials and PVA matrix (Bryaskova et al., 2011). For the nanomaterial-coated films, new peaks appeared at 2372, 2164, 2034, and 1983 cm–1, which can be attributed to the formation of new hydrogen bonds between MoS2, MoS2-NCQDs, or MoS2-Ag-NCQDs and the hydroxyl groups in the PVA matrix, respectively (Thakur et al., 2018). This bond formation plays a crucial role in enhancing the structural integrity and physical properties of these PVA-based nanocomposites (Yang et al., 2023). In particular, the decreased area of the peak corresponding to -OH stretching and the formation of the aforementioned new peaks for the nanomaterial-based films indicate that these nanomaterials were successfully coated on the surface of the PVA film.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was conducted to analyze the crystallographic structure of the PVA film and PVA/MoS2, PVA/MoS2-NCQD, and PVA/MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterial films and their dispersed homogeneity in the PVA matrix. Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns of pure PVA and PVA-based nanocomposite films. In the pure PVA film, a peak can be observed at 2θ = 19.3°, which contributes to the characteristic signals of the crystalline structure of PVA (101). The XRD patterns of the PVA/MoS2 and PVA/MoS2-NCQD nanomaterial films exhibit minor diffraction peaks corresponding to the (002) and (103) lattice planes of MoS2 nanosheets, which are observed at 2θ = 14.4°, along with a prominent PVA peak at 2θ = 19.3°. The XRD pattern of the PVA/MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterial film exhibits distinct characteristic peaks at 2θ = 38.1°, 44.1°, and 64.4° (JCPDS File No. 04–0783), which align well with the (111), (200), and (220) planes of Ag (Ansari, Singh, Ahmad, et al., 2019), respectively. Further, diffraction peaks at 2θ = 14.4° and 39.9° (JCPDS File No. 37–1492), corresponding to the (002) and (103) lattice planes of MoS2 nanosheets (Choi et al., 2024), respectively, and a prominent PVA peak at 2θ = 19.1° are observed (Jiang et al., 2014). The XRD pattern of the PVA/MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterial films shows a peak shift, with the 2θ values shifting from 19.3° to 19.1° upon the addition of Ag-NCQDs. This shift indicates that the Ag-NCQDs are substituted into the MoS2 lattice, increasing the d-spacing in the matrix and reducing the degree of detection. This observation indicates that the incorporated Ag-NCQDs effectively inhibit the restacking of MoS2 nanosheets. These findings indicate the successful incorporation of nanomaterials into the PVA matrices, resulting in alterations in the crystal structure, which can be attributed to hydrogen bonding and intermolecular entanglement between PVA and the incorporated nanomaterials. The percentage crystallinity of both pure PVA and PVA-based nanocomposite films can be quantitatively assessed by analyzing the areas under the crystalline and amorphous peaks in the XRD patterns (B. Aziz et al., 2020).

Here, A

Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) is a critical tool for examining the thermal behavior of films during both endothermic and exothermic processes. This technique enables a detailed analysis of thermal transitions that are essential during the heating phase, in which both the consumption and production of materials occur. Consequently, industries that utilize packaging materials must thoroughly understand the thermal properties of films to optimize their performance and ensure product integrity. The DSC thermograms of the pure PVA film and PVA/MoS2, PVA/MoS2-NCQD, and PVA/MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterial films are shown in Fig. 4 and the results are summarized in Table 1. The glass transition temperature (Tg) of PVA exhibits a noticeable increase from 85.5 ± 1.7–88.8 ± 1.2 °C upon the incorporation of nanomaterials. The T gs of the PVA/MoS2, PVA/MoS2-NCQD, and PVA/MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials are 87.1 ± 1.5, 87.9 ± 1.1, and 88.8 ± 1.2 °C, respectively. The increase in Tg associated with these nanomaterials is attributed to the constrained segmental mobility of the polymer chains at the interfaces. This phenomenon arises from the robust noncovalent interactions between the polymer and nanomaterials, which are enhanced by the extensive surface area presented by the 2D morphology of the nanomaterials (Ellison & Torkelson, 2003). This increase can be attributed to the formation of intermolecular hydrogen-bonding interactions between the added nanomaterials and PVA polymer chains (Lee et al., 2013). These findings indicate that the introduction of MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials leads to the formation of robust and enhanced interfacial interactions within the PVA matrix, suggesting improved compatibility and potential enhancements in the structural integrity and thermal properties of the material.

Further, the endothermic peaks exhibit a shift in the melting temperature (T m) from 191.1 ± 0.3–198.1 ± 0.7 °C for varying nanomaterials, which can be attributed to the change in the crystalline phase; moreover, the enthalpy (ΔH m) varied from 63.6 ± 2.1–73.7 ± 3.1 J/g. The degree of crystallinity increased from 13.9 % to 21.2 % with varying nanomaterials in the PVA matrix owing to the occurrence of intra- and intermolecular interactions (El-Shamy, 2019). The large surface area of the nanomaterial may create multiple nucleation sites, facilitating the formation of numerous nanoscale crystals with diverse size distributions. Consequently, this interaction led to a melting process that spanned a broader temperature range than that of a pure blend. The interaction between the incorporated nanomaterials and PVA chains resulted in a more organized and densely packed microstructure within the PVA matrix. This structural refinement was associated with a substantial increase in the overall crystallinity of the PVA-based nanocomposite films.

The incorporation of nanomaterials with high aspect ratios significantly influences the microstructural arrangement of polymer chains. Consequently, this alteration enhances the cohesiveness of the material and substantially improves the thermal stability of the nanomaterial film, making it more resistant to thermal variations, thereby expanding its potential application scope in various fields. The integration of nanomaterials into the PVA matrix significantly enhances the surface-to-volume ratio relative to that of bulk nanomaterials, which results in strong polymer–nanomaterial interactions. Consequently, the strong attractive forces between the nanomaterials reduce the mobility of the polymer segments, resulting in an elevated Tg, which exceeds that of the unmodified polymer. The degree of crystallinity within the polymer is determined on the basis of the heat of fusion/melting, which is obtained as follows (Carrera et al., 2013):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degrees of crystallinity of pure PVA and PVA-based nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: The crystallinity of the pure PVA and PVA-based nanocomposite films must be evaluated because the degree of crystallinity (χc) significantly affects the material properties of the polymer and improves the oxygen barrier performance. PVA, a semi-crystalline polymer, comprises two primary phases: crystalline and amorphous. Fig. S2 shows a comparative analysis of the crystallinity of the various nanomaterial films that were measured through XRD and DSC (Table 1). The degree of crystallinity is influenced by the inherent constraints of each characterization technique. XRD correlates the peak intensity and cumulative area from the diffractograms to establish the phase composition, which can be derived from either the individual diffraction peaks or the overall scattering pattern. In contrast, DSC determines crystallinity by comparing the enthalpy of fusion of the polymer matrix with that of a 100 % crystalline reference at its equilibrium melting point. Notably, the crystallinity of PVA is temperature dependent; thus, the DSC-derived crystallinity values at the melting temperature may differ from those measured under ambient conditions (Tarani et al., 2023). A combination of these techniques offers a comprehensive understanding of the structural attributes and crystallinity of the pure PVA and PVA-based nanocomposite films. Each method provides critical insights into crystallinity, and the PVA-based nanocomposite films show a trend of enhanced crystallinity. However, the crystallinity, calculated from the XRD and DSC data, increases consistently, indicating agreement between the results and confirming the enhancement of the PVA film properties due to the addition of nanomaterials.

The OTR and permeability coefficient (P) of packaging materials are critical factors in food preservation, particularly for oxygen-sensitive products. Gas permeability is influenced by several factors that impede the diffusion of gas molecules through polymer films. Notably, crystallinity plays a crucial role in reducing the permeation rates in polymers because increased crystallinity tends to restrict the mobility of gas molecules within a polymer matrix (Safandowska et al., 2022). A comparison of the OTR and P values of the pure PVA and PVA-based nanocomposite films reveals that the integration of nanomaterials into the PVA matrix obstructs the diffusion pathways for gas molecules ( Fig. 5(a) and (b); Table S1). Improved barrier properties are advantageous for extending the shelf life and maintaining product integrity. The OTR and permeability values for the PVA/MoS2 film are 0.29 ± 0.02 and 23.24 ± 1.3 cc.µm/m2·day, whereas those for pure PVA are 5.99 ± 0.1 and 474.79 ± 5.2 cc.µm/m2·day respectively. The unique reinforcing capabilities of MoS2, coupled with the polymeric matrix of PVA, facilitate the development of PVA/MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterial films that exhibit enhanced oxygen barrier performance (Shi et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2024). The mass transport of oxygen through barriers is intricately linked to the inherent attributes of the polymer, such as its free volume, porosity, and existence of voids. Notably, the crystallinity of a polymer plays a critical role in modulating its permeability; higher-crystallinity regions typically result in diminished solubility, thus creating a tortuous pathway that obstructs the diffusion of oxygen molecules (Nyflött et al., 2016). The OTR and P values of the PVA/MoS2 film are 95.2 % and 95.1 % lower than those of the pure PVA film, respectively. The oxygen barrier performance of the as-synthesized PVA/MoS2-NCQD and PVA/MoS2 Ag-NCQD films is higher than those reported for graphene-based PVA composites (Ashfaq et al., 2023). Furthermore, the OTR values of the PVA/MoS2-NCQD and PVA/MoS2-Ag-NCQD films are 99.7 % and 99.8 % lower than that of the pure PVA film, respectively. The permeability of these films also decreases significantly by 99.9 %, which can be attributed to the compact barrier layer formed by the exfoliated high-aspect-ratio MoS2 nanosheets. These MoS2 nanosheets create elongated and convoluted pathways that obstruct the diffusion of penetrants, such as O2 gas molecules, within the PVA-based nanocomposite films (Huang et al., 2012). The gas barrier performance significantly improves with the incorporation of different nanomaterials into the PVA matrix (Fig. 5). This enhancement can be linked to variations in the film thickness and optimization of the gas barrier properties associated with specific nanomaterial configurations. Moreover, the performance of gas barriers in semi-crystalline polymer-based nanocomposites is influenced by the highly crystalline nature of the incorporated nanomaterials (Thakur et al., 2018). The XRD and DSC results confirm that the crystallinity increases with variations in the nanomaterials.

A disruption in the packing of the polymer chain generates interstitial cavities, resulting in a fractional free volume. This increased free volume facilitates the diffusion of penetrants within the polymeric matrix as they navigate through these voids (Bandyopadhyay et al., 2013). The fractional free volume plays a critical role in optimizing the gas permeation characteristics of the polymer systems. The robust hydrogen-bonding interactions between the MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials and PVA improve the load transfer process, thus improving the oxygen barrier properties, thermal stability, and overall mechanical performance of the nanomaterials (Grunlan et al., 2004).

These results indicate that the incorporation of the MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials effectively decreases the fractional free volume within the polymer matrix by hindering polymer chain mobility and contributing to the formation of a packed structure with a high density. This alteration influences the permeability characteristics, particularly the solubility and diffusivity of gases within the polymer matrix (Bandyopadhyay et al., 2013). Particularly, the solubility of oxygen diminishes following the integration of MoS2-Ag-NCQDs, primarily because of the inherent insolubility of gas molecules in the MoS2 nanosheets (Compton et al., 2010). Furthermore, the introduced MoS2 nanosheets modify the flexibility of the PVA chains, thereby reducing the solubility of the gas molecules. Solubility is correlated to the distribution and characteristics of the nanomaterials embedded in the polymer matrix. Consequently, the gas molecules are compelled to traverse a long and tortuous pathway, and their movement is impeded by the impermeable, rigid, and densely packed MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials within the PVA matrix, leading to drastically reduced diffusivity (Fig. 5(c)) (Ren et al., 2017). The oxygen permeability coefficient is calculated as follows (Introzzi et al., 2012):

is the oxygen permeability coefficient (cc·μm/m2·day), Δp denotes the differential partial pressure of oxygen across the surfaces of the film, and t represents the total thickness encompassing both the substrate and coating layer.

The mechanical properties of the PVA-based nanocomposite films are crucial for their application in packaging. The tensile properties, illustrated through the typical stress–strain curves in Fig. 6(a) for PVA/MoS2, PVA/MoS2-NCQDs, and PVA/MoS2-Ag-NCQDs, were assessed using a universal testing machine (UTM). These films were subjected to tensile testing to evaluate parameters such as strength, plasticity, and elastic behavior, quantitatively represented by Young’s modulus, modulus of toughness, and modulus of resilience, as listed in Table 2. The incorporation of MoS2, MoS2-NCQD, and MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials significantly enhanced the tensile strength of the PVA matrix, with increases of 49.6 and 56.5 % for PVA/MoS2 and PVA/MoS2-NCQD nanomaterial films, respectively. Furthermore, with the addition of MoS2-Ag-NCQDs, the tensile strength further increased by 67.7 % (Fig. 6(b)). The pure PVA film exhibited classic ductile behavior, achieving a tensile strength of 25.4 MPa and an elongation at break of 123.8 %. As the nanomaterials varied, a linear decrease in the elongation at break was observed from (123.8 ± 2.9)% to (76.0 ± 1.0)% (Fig. 6(c)). This behavior can be attributed to the interactions between the nanomaterials and PVA chains, which led to molecular rearrangement and reordering within the amorphous regions of the PVA matrix, ultimately enhancing its crystallinity, as verified by the XRD and DSC results. The enhanced crystallinity, achieved through the incorporation of nanofillers, leads to the formation of a highly dense and more structured array within the PVA-based nanocomposite films. This structural improvement significantly improves the stiffness of the film, while maintaining precise molecular arrangement. The presence of nanofillers facilitates tight packing and alignment of polymer chains, resulting in enhanced mechanical integrity and performance. Consequently, these advancements contribute to the development of a more resilient material suitable for advanced applications (Ding et al., 2024). The uniform dispersion of the MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanosheets in the PVA matrix significantly improved the interfacial binding energy, thereby enhancing Young’s modulus by 80.2 % with that of pure PVA (Fig. 6(d)). The mechanical properties of the PVA-based nanocomposite films were significantly dependent on the nanomaterials. All films exhibited a specific trend, with a maximum strength of 78.92 ± 3.0 MPa, in the case of MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials. The mechanical properties of the MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterial films were observed to depend on the high aspect ratios of the PVA matrix.

The modulus of resilience measures the ability of a material to absorb energy elastically before yielding and is represented as energy per unit volume. This property is derived from the area beneath the elastic segment of the stress-strain curve of the material, which represents the energy density that can be stored elastically. The incorporation of MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials led to a notable enhancement in the elastic characteristics from 54.0 ± 2.3–135.7 ± 5.1 MPa (Fig. 6(e)). Toughness reflects the capacity of a material to absorb energy before fracturing or rupturing. In a uniform specimen, the modulus of toughness is determined by the area under the stress-strain curve that is obtained from a tensile test, which represents the strain energy per unit area up to the point of failure (Oh, 2022). The modulus of toughness demonstrated a significant enhancement with the incorporation of nanomaterials, particularly MoS2-Ag-NCQDs, which achieved a modulus of 3000.9 ± 4.9 MPa—47.5 % higher than that of pure PVA (Fig. 6(f)). The mechanism for the enhancement of the mechanical properties of the PVA-based nanocomposite films was attributed to the dense barrier layer formed by the MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials. Furthermore, the interaction mechanism arising from the inhomogeneous distribution of the MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials significantly enhanced the tensile strength and Young’s modulus within the PVA matrix. This underscores the pivotal role of hydrogen bonds in establishing strong interfacial interactions when these components are integrated into pure PVA.

Transparency and optical properties are important for maintaining the visibility of packaging films to satisfy consumer demand. Active nanomaterials are known to change the appearance of polymer-based active films by altering their absorption properties. Fig. S3 shows the absorption spectra of the pure PVA, PVA/MoS2, PVA/MoS2-NCQD, PVA/Ag-NCQD, and PVA/MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterial films. Fig. 7(a) shows the transmittance profiles of the pure PVA and PVA-based nanocomposite films. The analysis revealed that these nanomaterial films effectively absorbed UV light while maintaining transparency in the visible spectrum, as indicated by their transmittance spectra. The incorporation of MoS2 into the PVA matrix significantly enhances the ability of the film to attenuate UV light, effectively reducing the transmittance of harmful UV radiation. The PVA/MoS2 film demonstrates a UV-blocking capability of 21.6 %, whereas the PVA/Ag-NCQD and PVA/MoS2-Ag-NCQD variants exhibited superior performance, blocking approximately 93.1 % and 83.5 % of UV-B radiation, respectively. The PVA/Ag-NCQD composite exhibited outstanding performance, effectively obstructing approximately 93.1 % of UV-B radiation. This impressive protection performance can be attributed to the distinctive characteristics of the Ag-NCQDs, which significantly contribute to their ability to enhance UV shielding. The innovative properties of these quantum dots enhance the composite’s functionality as well as highlight their potential for advanced applications in UV protection technologies (Ansari et al., 2025; Silva et al., 2024). Conversely, the PVA/MoS2-NCQD film blocked 59.4 % of UV light. The strategic amalgamation of these nanomaterials into the PVA matrix resulted in the production of advanced UV-blocking films characterized by superior transparency and flexibility. This enhancement in UV absorption can be attributed to the increased interaction of the UV-active MoS2-Ag-NCQDs with the PVA film, solidifying their potential for applications requiring UV protection without compromising visible light transmittance.

Photographs of the pure PVA and PVA-based nanocomposite films are shown in Fig. 7(b). These images reveal that the film maintains high transparency to visible light. Notably, the transparencies of the PVA films are largely consistent, showing minimal variation despite changes in experimental conditions. The PVA/MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterial film exhibited an impressive optical transparency of 96.7 % while effectively decreasing UV transmittance by 83.5 % in the UV-B region incorporated in the PVA/MoS2 matrix. This significant reduction in UV transmission is primarily attributed to the innate UV-absorption capabilities of the Ag-NCQDs, which efficiently block UV-B radiation. These PVA-based nanocomposite films, which strike a balance between high transparency and robust UV shielding, are ideal for packaging applications, particularly for products that are vulnerable to UV degradation.

This study investigated the UV-blocking efficacy of various PVA-based nanocomposite films and pure PVA films using ginger as the test substrate ( Fig. 8). Exposure to UV radiation induces the formation of reactive oxygen species, which can compromise membrane integrity, degrade chlorophyll, and accelerate cellular senescence (Zhao et al., 2022). The empirical findings indicated that the ginger samples encased in the control and pure PVA films exhibited notable brown discoloration and dehydration. Conversely, the ginger samples shielded by PVA-based nanocomposite films maintained their structural integrity and color more effectively. PVA-based nanocomposite films achieved a substantial 79.4 % reduction in UV exposure, underscoring their ability as potent UV-protective barriers for food packaging applications with the potential to significantly enhance the shelf life of UV-sensitive products.

MoS2, MoS2-NCQD, and MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials were successfully synthesized using ultrasonic mixing, liquid exfoliation, and hydrothermal techniques and their application scope in PVA-based packaging films was verified. The incorporation of MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterials into the PVA matrix resulted in significant enhancements in crystallinity, mechanical properties, and oxygen barrier performance. The OTR value decreased significantly from 5.99 ± 0.14–0.008 ± 0.0 cc/m²·day, indicating that MoS2-Ag-NCQDs efficiently impeded oxygen diffusion, thus significantly improving the barrier properties of the PVA film. Mechanical testing showed a substantial increase in tensile strength from 25.4 ± 1.2–78.9 ± 3.0 MPa for the PVA/MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterial films, reflecting enhanced structural integrity. Storage evaluation underscored the potential of the PVA/MoS2-Ag-NCQD nanomaterial films as effective UV-shielding materials. The films demonstrated an impressive decrease of approximately 79.4 % in the penetration of UV radiation, thereby reducing the oxidative degradation that is typically linked to UV exposure in stored fruits. The large surface area of the MoS2 nanosheets contributed considerably to the improved UV shielding and oxygen barrier performance of the PVA-based nanocomposite films. The findings of this study suggest that the use of PVA-based nanocomposite films, particularly those incorporating MoS2-Ag-NCQDs, represents a promising strategy for food packaging applications that aim to preserve food quality and extend the shelf life of oxygen-sensitive food products.
